immediate_actions: Eliminate all ignition sources within 300 feet
Evacuate downwind personnel immediately
Do not attempt to extinguish unless trained
Contain spill with absorbent material if safe to do so
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air immediately
If not breathing, begin CPR
Remove contaminated clothing
Flush skin with water for at least 20 minutes
fire_response: Use dry chemical, CO2, or alcohol-resistant foam. Water may be ineffective.
spill_response: Eliminate ignition sources. Absorb with earth or sand. Prevent entry into sewers.

immediate_actions: Evacuate area -- minimum 1500 feet in all directions
Move upwind immediately
Do NOT apply water directly to leak
Isolate spill area and restrict access
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air immediately
If breathing difficulty, administer oxygen
Remove contaminated clothing
If eyes exposed, flush with water for 15 minutes
fire_response: Chlorine is not flammable but supports combustion. Use water spray to reduce vapors.
spill_response: Do not direct water at source. Use fine water spray to knock down vapors.

ppe_required: Level A or B Hazmat Suit
immediate_actions: Evacuate area -- minimum 1000 feet
Stay upwind
Ammonia is lighter than air and rises
Isolate the area and deny entry
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air
If not breathing, begin artificial respiration
Flush contaminated skin with large amounts of water
If eyes exposed, irrigate with water for 15 minutes minimum
fire_response: Ammonia containers may rocket if heated. Cool containers with water from maximum distance.
spill_response: Use fine water spray to absorb vapors. Do not direct water into liquid ammonia.

immediate_actions: Eliminate all ignition sources within 500 feet
Propane vapor is heavier than air -- collects in low areas
Evacuate downhill and downwind
Close cylinder valve if safe to do so
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air
Administer oxygen if breathing difficulty
Treat frostbite if exposure to liquid propane
Begin CPR if not breathing
fire_response: Do not extinguish unless leak can be stopped. Cool containers with water from distance.
spill_response: Stop leak if possible without risk. Ventilate area. Keep out of low areas.

immediate_actions: Isolate spill area
Do not touch spilled material
Neutralize small spills with baking soda or lime
Prevent runoff from entering drains or waterways
first_aid: Flush skin with water for at least 30 minutes
Remove contaminated clothing while flushing
If swallowed, do NOT induce vomiting
Flush eyes with water for at least 30 minutes
fire_response: Acid is not flammable. Water spray may produce toxic fumes. Use dry chemical.
spill_response: Absorb with dry earth or sand. Neutralize with soda ash. Do not use sawdust.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources within 200 feet
Ventilate the area
Acetone vapors are heavier than air
Prevent entry into confined spaces
first_aid: Move to fresh air if inhaled
Flush eyes with water for 15 minutes
Wash skin with soap and water
If ingested, do not induce vomiting -- seek medical attention
fire_response: Use alcohol-resistant foam, dry chemical, or CO2. Water spray may be ineffective.
spill_response: Absorb with inert material. Ventilate area. Prevent entry into sewers.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources
Ventilate the area
Contain spill with absorbent material
Avoid breathing vapors
first_aid: Move to fresh air
Flush eyes with water for 15 minutes
Wash skin with soap and water
If ingested, do not induce vomiting
fire_response: Use dry chemical, CO2, or alcohol-resistant foam.
spill_response: Contain with absorbent material. Prevent entry into waterways.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources within 200 feet
Contain spill if safe
Prevent entry into drains and waterways
Ventilate enclosed areas
first_aid: Move to fresh air
Flush eyes with water for 15 minutes
Wash skin with soap and water
If ingested, do not induce vomiting
fire_response: Use dry chemical, foam, or CO2. Water fog can be used for cooling.
spill_response: Absorb with earth, sand, or commercial absorbent. Prevent waterway contamination.

immediate_actions: Evacuate area -- minimum 300 feet
Stay upwind
Do not touch spilled material
Isolate area and deny entry
first_aid: Move to fresh air immediately
Flush skin with water for 20 minutes
Flush eyes with water for 30 minutes
If inhaled, administer oxygen
fire_response: Not flammable. May produce toxic chlorine gas when heated. Use water spray to cool containers.
spill_response: Neutralize with soda ash or lime. Absorb residue with dry material.

immediate_actions: Keep combustible materials away from oxygen source
Oxygen accelerates combustion -- even non-flammable materials may ignite
Ventilate enclosed areas
Move containers away from fire if safe
first_aid: Move victim away from oxygen-enriched atmosphere
Treat for burns if ignition occurred
If frostbite from liquid oxygen, warm gradually with lukewarm water
fire_response: Water spray or fog. Do NOT use oil-based agents near oxygen.
spill_response: Ventilate area. Keep combustibles away. No smoking or open flames.

immediate_actions: Eliminate all ignition sources within 150 feet
Ventilate area thoroughly
Prevent entry into drains or sewers
Contain spill with dry sand or absorbent material
first_aid: Skin contact: wash with soap and water for 15 minutes
Eye contact: flush with water for 15 minutes, seek medical attention
Inhalation: move to fresh air, oxygen if breathing difficulty
Ingestion: do NOT induce vomiting, call Poison Control
fire_response: Use CO2, dry chemical, or alcohol-resistant foam.
spill_response: Absorb with inert dry material. Keep away from heat and ignition sources.

immediate_actions: Evacuate 300 feet in all directions
Shut off gas supply if safe to do so
Eliminate ALL ignition sources — acetylene has wide explosive range
Cool cylinders with water from maximum safe distance
Do NOT move heated or damaged cylinders
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air immediately
Administer oxygen if breathing difficulty
CPR if not breathing
Treat for frostbite if exposed to cold gas release
fire_response: Do NOT extinguish unless flow can be stopped. Cool surrounding area with water.
spill_response: Protect surrounding buildings from blast. Isolate until gas dissipates.

immediate_actions: SCBA required — CO is colorless and odorless, immediately life-threatening
Evacuate all personnel from the area immediately
Eliminate ignition sources
Ventilate enclosed spaces before entry
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air IMMEDIATELY — CO displaces oxygen in blood
Administer 100% oxygen and keep victim warm and at rest
Unconscious victim: CPR if not breathing, call for ALS
All CO exposure requires hospital evaluation for carboxyhemoglobin levels
fire_response: If flow cannot be stopped, do not extinguish — CO will re-accumulate. Cool containers.
spill_response: Ventilate area. Continuous air monitoring required. CO detectors mandatory.

immediate_actions: Eliminate all ignition sources
Evacuate non-essential personnel
Methanol vapor is invisible — use gas detector
Prevent runoff into sewers
first_aid: Skin: remove contaminated clothing, wash with soap and water 15 minutes
Eyes: flush with water 15 minutes, immediate medical attention
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen, call ALS
Ingestion: call Poison Control IMMEDIATELY — methanol causes blindness and death. Do NOT induce vomiting
fire_response: Alcohol-resistant foam, CO2, dry chemical. Water may spread fire.
spill_response: Contain with sand or earth. Flush area with water. Do not allow entry into waterways.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources within 150 feet
Ventilate area
Contain spill — ethanol is water-miscible
first_aid: Skin: wash with water
Eyes: flush with water 15 minutes
Inhalation: fresh air
Ingestion: call Poison Control — high concentrations are toxic
fire_response: Alcohol-resistant foam preferred. CO2 or dry chemical acceptable.
spill_response: Dilute with large amounts of water. Keep away from ignition sources.

immediate_actions: Evacuate 500 feet in all directions
Eliminate ALL ignition sources
Liquid ethylene boils at -104°C — cryogenic burns on contact
Do not touch liquid or frosted surfaces
first_aid: Cryogenic burns: gently warm with lukewarm (not hot) water
Do NOT rub frozen tissue
Immerse in warm water 40-42°C
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen
fire_response: Do not extinguish unless flow can be stopped. Cool surrounding surfaces with water fog.
spill_response: Stop leak from safe distance. Allow vapor cloud to disperse.

immediate_actions: Do NOT allow water to contact concentrated sulfuric acid — violent spattering
Evacuate downwind — acid fumes are toxic
Contain spill with dry sand or lime
Neutralize with sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) only after containment
first_aid: Skin: remove clothing immediately, flush with LARGE amounts of water for 20 minutes
Eyes: flush with water 20-30 minutes, EMERGENCY — acid causes permanent blindness
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen, hospital
Ingestion: do NOT induce vomiting, dilute with water or milk, hospital immediately
fire_response: Acid does not burn but reacts violently with water and organics. Use dry sand or earth.
spill_response: Restrict access. Neutralize with lime or sodium bicarbonate. Do not use water directly.

immediate_actions: Keep away from all combustibles — concentrated H2O2 causes spontaneous ignition
Evacuate area of all combustible materials
Do NOT allow to contact clothing, wood, leather, or organic material
first_aid: Skin: flush with large amounts of water for 20 minutes
Eyes: flush with water 20 minutes, immediate medical attention
Inhalation: fresh air, seek medical attention
Concentrated H2O2 causes oxygen embolism if ingested — do NOT induce vomiting, hospital immediately
fire_response: Cool containers with water. Flood area with water to dilute. AVOID contact with combustibles.
spill_response: Dilute with large amounts of water. Keep away from organic material and combustibles.

immediate_actions: Evacuate non-essential personnel — benzene is a known carcinogen
Eliminate ignition sources within 300 feet
SCBA required — benzene vapors are toxic and carcinogenic
Prevent runoff into water sources
first_aid: Skin: remove contaminated clothing, wash with soap and water
Eyes: flush with water 15 minutes
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen, hospital — benzene causes CNS depression
All exposures should receive medical evaluation for benzene toxicity
fire_response: Foam, CO2, dry chemical. Do not use water jet — spreads fire.
spill_response: Contain spill. Absorb with inert material. Recover for disposal as hazardous waste.

ppe_required: SCBA — EO is immediately dangerous to life and health
immediate_actions: EVACUATE 1000 feet immediately — ethylene oxide is highly toxic and flammable
SCBA mandatory — EO is odorless at dangerous concentrations
Eliminate ignition sources — explosive range 3-100%
Do NOT re-enter without air monitoring
first_aid: Move victim to fresh air IMMEDIATELY
Remove all contaminated clothing
Flush all exposed skin with water for 20 minutes
All exposures require hospital evaluation — EO is a carcinogen and causes delayed effects
fire_response: Alcohol-resistant foam. Water spray to knock down vapors. Do NOT enter without SCBA.
spill_response: Isolate area. Monitor with gas detector. Allow to evaporate with maximum ventilation.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ALL ignition sources — hydrogen has the widest explosive range of any gas (4-75%)
Evacuate 500 feet — hydrogen flames are nearly invisible in daylight
Cool cylinders with water from maximum distance
Do NOT move heated cylinders
first_aid: Move to fresh air — hydrogen displaces oxygen causing asphyxiation
Treat for asphyxiation if unconscious
Treat burns if fire occurred
fire_response: Do NOT extinguish unless flow can be stopped — unburned hydrogen will re-accumulate explosively.
spill_response: Isolate area. Ventilate. Use gas detector — hydrogen flames are invisible.

immediate_actions: Evacuate downwind — HCl fumes are toxic and corrosive to lungs
Do NOT breathe vapors
Contain spill — prevent entry into waterways
Neutralize with lime or soda ash only after containment
first_aid: Skin: remove clothing, flush with water 20 minutes
Eyes: flush with water 20 minutes — emergency
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen, hospital — HCl causes pulmonary edema
Ingestion: dilute with water, hospital immediately
fire_response: HCl does not burn. Use water spray to disperse vapors. Cool containers.
spill_response: Ventilate area. Neutralize with lime after containment. Prevent runoff.

immediate_actions: Prevent material from entering waterways, storm drains, or soil
Contain spill with dry material
Identify specific substance if possible — hazards vary widely
first_aid: Avoid skin contact
Wash exposed areas with soap and water
If ingestion or inhalation, identify substance and call Poison Control
fire_response: Respond based on specific substance properties. Use water unless contraindicated.
spill_response: Contain and prevent environmental spread. Contact hazmat team for cleanup.

immediate_actions: Eliminate all ignition sources within 300 feet
Evacuate downwind — crude oil vapors are toxic
Contain large spills — crude is an environmental hazard
Do not allow entry into storm drains
first_aid: Skin: remove clothing, wash with soap and water
Eyes: flush with water 15 minutes
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen if breathing difficulty
All inhalation exposures require medical evaluation
fire_response: Foam, dry chemical. Cool surrounding tanks with water. Do not use water jet on spill.
spill_response: Contain with berms. Recover product for disposal. Environmental notification required.

ppe_required: SCBA — bromine is immediately dangerous to life
immediate_actions: EVACUATE 1000 feet — bromine vapor is extremely toxic (IDLH: 3 ppm)
SCBA mandatory
Move upwind and uphill
Notify hazmat team immediately
first_aid: Move to fresh air immediately
Remove ALL contaminated clothing
Flush skin with large amounts of water for 20 minutes
Eyes: flush with water 20 minutes — bromine causes severe burns
All exposures require hospital evaluation
fire_response: Bromine is not flammable but supports combustion. Water spray to suppress vapors.
spill_response: Do NOT attempt cleanup without Level A protection. Contain and notify hazmat.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources
Evacuate area — acetonitrile metabolizes to cyanide in the body
SCBA required — vapors are toxic
Prevent spill from entering waterways
first_aid: Skin: remove clothing, wash with soap and water
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen, hospital — risk of cyanide poisoning
Eyes: flush 15 minutes
All exposures require hospital evaluation due to cyanide risk
fire_response: Alcohol-resistant foam, CO2, dry chemical.
spill_response: Contain. Ventilate area. Recover with inert absorbent.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources — acetone has very low flash point (-18°C)
Ventilate area thoroughly
Contain spill with dry sand or absorbent material
first_aid: Skin: wash with soap and water
Eyes: flush with water 15 minutes
Inhalation: fresh air — high concentrations cause CNS depression
Ingestion: do NOT induce vomiting, call Poison Control
fire_response: Alcohol-resistant foam, CO2, dry chemical.
spill_response: Remove ignition sources. Absorb with inert material. Ventilate area.

immediate_actions: Eliminate ignition sources
Evacuate downwind
Prevent vapor accumulation in low areas
Contain spill
first_aid: Skin: wash with soap and water
Eyes: flush with water 15 minutes
Inhalation: fresh air, oxygen if needed
All inhalation exposures should be medically evaluated
fire_response: Foam, CO2, dry chemical. Do not use water jet.
spill_response: Absorb with inert material. Prevent entry into drains.
